title: VIGIL
byline: Listening station VIGIL. The crew didn't leave. They're just not here.

bunk.title: Crew bunk 3
bunk.text:
You surface from sleep the way a diver comes up too fast -- ears ringing, the taste of recycled air gone sour on your tongue. The bunk light is the only thing burning, a thin red bar above your head, and the station around it holds the particular hush of a held breath.

VIGIL is a listening post. Its whole purpose is to be quiet and patient out here at the lip of nowhere, an ear pressed to the dark. The shift roster on the wall says eleven souls aboard. The clock says you slept four hours past your watch. Nobody woke you. Nobody is talking on the comm. Down the rack, every other bunk is made up neat and cold, and the only sound is the slow tick of the station settling, on and off, like it is counting.

choices.text: Sit up slowly and put your feet on the deck.
Lie still a moment and just listen to the station.

bunk_listen.title: Crew bunk 3
bunk_listen.text:
You stay flat and let the place speak. The air handler breathes. A loose panel ticks. And under all of it, far off down the spine of the station, something moves -- a soft, dragging weight that pauses the instant you notice it, as if it were also listening, as if your attention had reached down the corridor and touched it.

You decide you have listened enough.

choices.text: Get up and put your feet on the deck.

bunk_up.title: Crew bunk 3
bunk_up.text: The deck is cold through your socks. On the wall a personal locker hangs open, somebody's name half-worn off the tape. Inside, a med-gel ampoule and a strip of dermal tabs -- the kind issued for when a watch runs long and a mind starts to fray at the edge. You take the gel. Past the bunkroom hatch a short corridor runs to the station's central ring.

choices.text: Pocket the med-gel and go to the ring.
Leave it and go to the ring.

ring.title: Central ring
ring.text:
The ring is the heart of VIGIL, a circular junction where every section meets. Workstations sit at their odd hour of abandonment -- a coffee bulb still strapped in its clip, a glove left palm-up on a keyboard, a chair turned to face the corridor mouth as though its occupant stood and walked toward something. There are no bodies and there are no notes -- only the shape of people who were here a moment ago and are not here now.

Three sections open off the ring. The comms array curves away to port, where the station does its listening. The crew quarters and galley lie aft. And forward, past a heavy pressure hatch, the launch bay holds the evac pod -- and the only way out is across its open floor.

sanityText.62: The ring has stopped pretending to be a room. The coffee bulb sways though nothing moves the air. The turned chair is facing you now; you are fairly sure it was not. Each workstation feels occupied by the suggestion of a person, all of them attending to the same thing, which is you. Three sections open off the ring. You try not to look directly at any of them.

choices.text: Comms array (port).
Crew quarters and galley (aft).
The launch bay hatch (forward).

comms.title: Comms array
comms.text:
The array room is a long gallery of receivers, every dish on the hull feeding down to this dim warren of screens. Most are dead. One still scrolls a waveform, patient and slow. The crew spent their lives in here straining to hear a whisper from very far away, and somewhere in the last few hours the listening turned the other way around.

A maintenance rack against the bulkhead holds a row of charged power cells -- the heavy kind, rated to spin up a pod's drive. One sits ready in its cradle, a green charge bar steady in the gloom. The console in the center of the room offers a distress protocol, a single key away from broadcasting a loop into the dark.

choices.text: Lift the charged power cell from the rack.
Key the distress protocol and broadcast a loop.
Read the last received signal on the live screen.
Back to the ring.

comms_signal.title: Comms array
comms_signal.text: The live screen holds the last thing VIGIL heard. It is not a transmission. It is a recording the station made of its own room -- this room -- timestamped two hours ago, and on it the waveform spikes and settles in a slow rhythm that is not machinery and is not speech. It is the sound of breathing. The room was empty when it was recorded. You know this because you can see, at the edge of the log, that the only motion-sensor that tripped was the one tracking your own bunk, four sections away, where you lay asleep and were being listened to.

choices.text: Kill the screen and step back.

quarters.title: Crew quarters
quarters.text:
The quarters are worse than empty -- they are mid-sentence. A meal sits half-eaten and long cold in the galley, a fork laid down beside it with care. Photos are pinned above bunks. A game of some kind is frozen on a table, pieces mid-move, one player's hand-knit blanket still holding the dent of a body that rose and did not sit back down.

At the far end, one crewman did not vanish clean. There is no body, but there is the place a body fought -- a bunk frame bent outward, a panel scored deep, and on the bulkhead beside it, scratched into the paint with something hard and desperate, a line of letters. A maintenance kit lies dropped beneath it, spilled, a med-gel ampoule rolled into the corner.

choices.text: Take the med-gel from the spilled kit.
Read the scratched warning on the bulkhead.
Back to the ring.

quarters_warning.title: Crew quarters
quarters_warning.text:
You make yourself read it, letter by gouged letter. The hand that cut it was failing toward the end, the lines going loose and wild, but the message holds:

"IT WORKS BY YOUR LOOKING. THE BAY IS OPEN GROUND AND IT WATCHES THERE. DON'T LOOK AT IT, DON'T LOOK AT THE POD, KEEP YOUR EYES DOWN AND CROSS SLOW. IT ONLY HAS YOU WHEN YOU GIVE IT YOUR EYES."

Below the words, a single shaky arrow points at the deck, at your own feet. Down. Always down. You understand, now, the turned chairs and the listening room. The thing aboard VIGIL does not hunt by sound or scent. It hunts by being noticed. To attend to it is to hand it the leash.

choices.text: Step back from the wall, eyes on the floor.

bay.title: Launch bay hatch
bay.text:
The pressure hatch unseals with a sigh and you are looking into the launch bay -- a wide, cold cathedral of a room, its far wall a blast door, and across forty meters of bare deck the evac pod waiting in its cradle. Between you and it there is nothing -- no cover, no console to crouch behind, just the open floor, and the sense, immediate and total, that the open floor is not empty, that the emptiness has a weight to it and a patience, and that it has registered the hatch coming open.

The pod's cradle console will want a charge to spin the drive. The forty meters in between will want something else from you.

sanityText.62: The hatch opens on the bay and the bay is already aware of it. Forty meters of bare deck, the pod a small bright promise at the far end, and the air over the floor bent somehow, thick the way water is thick, full of an attention that turns toward the open hatch like a face you refuse to find. It wants your eyes. You can feel exactly where it is by how badly you want to look.

choices.text: Cross with your eyes down, slow and unlooking, the way the warning said.
Cross with your eyes down, slow and unlooking.
choices.locked: Cross to the pod. (no power cell -- the pod's drive is dead)

choices.text: Cross the open floor as fast as you can to the pod.
Haul the blast door open to be sure of the way out first.
Back away and seal the hatch.

bay_ran.title: Launch bay
bay_ran.text: You break for the pod at a dead sprint, boots cracking on the deck, and every meter of it is a mistake. Speed is motion and motion is a kind of shout. The weight in the room turns its whole attention on you at once, a pressure that closes around your skull like a fist, and the forty meters stretch and warp until the pod seems to recede ahead of you. You reach the cradle gasping, something vast and unseen now standing very close at your back, no longer patient. You did not keep your eyes down. It knows exactly where you are.

choices.text: Get into the pod and seal it -- if there is power to fly.
Claw at the dead console -- no cell, no charge, nothing.

cross_unseen.title: Launch bay
cross_unseen.text: You fix your eyes on the seam between two deck plates and you walk. Not fast. Slow as a man wading a current, each footfall set down soft, your gaze nailed to the floor a meter ahead. The weight in the room slides past you, around you, searching -- you feel it pass close enough to stir the hair on your arms -- but you do not give it your eyes, you do not look at it and you do not look at the pod, you only watch the next plate and the next. It cannot find the edges of you if you do not draw them for it. Forty meters of the hardest stillness of your life, and then your outstretched hand finds the cold lip of the cradle.

choices.text: Seat the power cell and board the pod, eyes still down.

ending.text:
You board with your eyes on the floor and you keep them there while your fingers find the launch key by feel. The cradle releases. Only when VIGIL is a cold grey ring shrinking in the port do you let yourself look up -- and even then you cannot say whether the shadow that uncoils along the station's spine to watch you go is real, or only the shape your fear leaves on the dark.

You are awake. You are alone. You did not look, and so you are alive.

You slam the cell home and the drive catches, a rising whine. The hatch irises shut a breath before the thing at your back arrives, and you feel it strike the hull as the pod tears free, one enormous blow, denied. VIGIL falls away into the black. You made it -- breathing, heart slamming, the drive sound under your feet.

But the quiet in the pod is the wrong quiet, the listening kind. You crossed that floor with your eyes wide and your mind thrown open, and it had a long, clear look at you. When you close your eyes you are back on the deck with the weight bending toward you, and you know it kept a thread of you and will reel it in slow, across whatever distance, for as long as you have a mind left to be found in. You got out. You will never again be entirely alone, and that is worse.

You key the loop and VIGIL begins to shout its position into the dark, over and over, a hand thrust up out of deep water. Something takes hold of it. The thing was already aboard, but a broadcast is the loudest looking there is -- the whole station reaching out, naming itself, begging to be noticed -- and the noticing comes straight back down the same wire. The lights in the comms room dim toward you one bank at a time. You do not get to finish the second loop.

You set your shoulder to the blast door and haul, and it grinds back on the stars, and for one foolish triumphant second you are looking straight out at the way home. Which means you are looking. The open floor behind you has your full attention now, turned the wrong way, your back to forty meters of patient weight. You hear it cross the deck without hurry. There is no point turning around. Turning around is exactly what it wants, and you have already given it everything else.

The console is dead and there is no cell to wake it and you are forty meters out on open ground with your eyes wide and your mind a flare. The pod will not fly. The room has all of you now, every edge of you drawn bright for it, and it takes its time, because patience was always the whole of it. You should have kept your eyes down. You should have brought the charge. You did neither, and the dark folds shut.
